question.text: Scenario: Mr. Ramesh is 55 years old and has been employed with a manufacturing company for 28 years. His current basic salary is Rs. 80,000 per month and dearness allowance (DA) is Rs. 20,000 per month. He is covered under the Payment of Gratuity Act. His EPF balance stands at Rs. 45,00,000 (employee share Rs. 22,00,000, employer share Rs. 23,00,000). He also has an NPS Tier I account with a total corpus of Rs. 32,00,000 (own contributions: Rs. 16,00,000) and a superannuation fund with Rs. 18,00,000. His employer contributes 12% to EPF, 10% of basic+DA to NPS, and Rs. 1,50,000 annually to the superannuation fund. He is considering various pre-retirement withdrawal options and wants to understand his retirement benefits. He has never taken an NPS partial withdrawal before. Question 3 of 5: Mr. Ramesh wants to take a partial withdrawal from his NPS Tier I account now (at age 55). What is the maximum amount he can withdraw, and what are the conditions?

answer.text: Rs. 4,00,000 (25% of own contributions of Rs. 16,00,000); he must meet the specified purpose criteria, and this would be his first of maximum 4 withdrawals allowed before age 60
Rs. 8,00,000 (25% of total corpus of Rs. 32,00,000); no restrictions on purpose
Rs. 4,00,000 (25% of own contributions); but he cannot withdraw since he needs a minimum 4-year gap which would take him past age 60
He cannot take any partial withdrawal since he is above age 54 and should wait for normal exit at 60

explanation.text:
**The Logic:** NPS Tier I allows partial withdrawal of up to 25% of the subscriber's own contributions (not total corpus). With own contributions of Rs. 16,00,000, the maximum is Rs. 4,00,000. He must have completed at least 3 years of membership (he has 28 years of employment). A maximum of 4 partial withdrawals are allowed before age 60 with a minimum 4-year gap between each. This is his first, so the gap rule doesn't apply yet. The withdrawal must be for specified purposes (education, marriage, illness, house purchase, etc.) and is tax-exempt.
**The Trap:** Calculating 25% on total corpus instead of own contributions is the classic NPS trap. The option about the 4-year gap preventing withdrawal is wrong — the gap applies between successive withdrawals, not the first one. There is no restriction on partial withdrawal based on proximity to age 60.
**Cross-Exam Context:** NPS partial withdrawal rules are among the most tested topics in NISM X-B. The key parameters — 25%, own contributions, 3-year membership, 4 withdrawals max, 4-year gap — must all be remembered precisely.
**Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Since Mr. Ramesh has never taken a partial withdrawal, he has all 4 available. At 55, if he takes one now, the 4-year gap means the next would be at 59 (still before 60). Plan withdrawals strategically to maximize liquidity while preserving the retirement corpus.
